---
title: "Financial News Sentimental Analysis and their Correlation with Daily Trade Volume"
author: "Ifa Bato, Ashton Kabou, Gene Ni, Nebeyu Tsigea, Rachel Wang"
subtitle: "Final project for INST447, Spring 2026"
format: html
---
# Executive Summary

This project is designed for investors who hold positions in publicly traded stocks, particularly focused on Apple (AAPL). It may be hard to consistently monitor the constant stream of financial news, analyst opinions, and quantitative commentary that surrounds their investments. Rather than tracking prices, which investors can observe directly, our tool focuses on the sentiment landscape of the news and public conversations around the stock, giving the users a quick pulse on whether the coverage is heading towards positive, negative, or neutral and what stocks lead in that direction.
The primary data source is the Finnhub financial news API, which looks at articles from major outlets including Yahoo Finance, Benzinga, SeekingAlpha, CNBC, ChartMill, and Finnhub's own feed. The trade volume data is pulled from Yahoo Finance from the yfinance library. The trade volume is used to compare if positive or negative sentiment has an impact on the trade volume of AAPL.

The key findings were from the 150+ news articles over May 1–2, 2026 using the FinBERT model; it skewed meaningfully positive, driven by Apple’s blowout fiscal Q2 2026 earnings report, which recorded a revenue of $111.2B. This was caused by a 22% surge in iPhone sales, a $100B share buyback, and beating Wall Street estimates. Neutral was the second most common label, showing market context articles that mentioned AAPL without taking a specific stance. Negative sentiment was a minority but clustered around real concerns, such as the “RAMageddon”, the supply constraints of Mac items, and geopolitical tariff risks. A notable bias finding was source concentration, Yahoo Finance dominated the dataset by volume, which means the way they edit their articles largely shapes the overall sentiment distribution. FinBERT confidence was generally high (median above 0.87), with lower confidence observed on mixed or ambiguous articles, validating the model’s calibration on financial text. Along with that there is strong evidence of the news sentiment following the overall trend of the stock. Meaning negative sentiment means overall less trades, and high sentiment pushes for higher trade volume. As AAPL is an overall bullish stock, the high confidence of traders means that negative sentiment usually causes hesitation and not a sell off.

The most technically challenging aspect was trying to integrate the FinBert pipeline to work with the full ETL workflow. It was really difficult to manage the limits, and cleaning the characters, and handling API summaries that cut off mid sentence. It was also hard to get started, trying to find a stock that had a multitude of sources that weren’t all biased was something that took time to complie. It was also very interesting to determine how to handle articles that mentioned AAPL broadly, especially since they were more focused on the market rather than Apple specifically. The source bias analysis really highlighted how diverse the dataset is yet still so concentrated with so much data.

Project library imports:
```{python}
import requests
from dotenv import load_dotenv
import yfinance as yf
import os
from transformers import pipeline
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import time
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
```

Load environment variables:
```{python}
load_dotenv()
```

# Source(s) & Extraction

We are extracting data from two main sources: yFinance (https://ranaroussi.github.io/yfinance/) and Finnhub Market News API (https://finnhub.io/docs/api/market-news). Yahoo! finance data is extracted directly from the package’s underlying requests to Yahoo’s api. You can view the data without the API from Yahoo Finance (example with AAPL - https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/AAPL/). Finnhub News API obtained their data through either contracts with news companies, or they obtained it through public databases and scraping it themselves. But Looking at their professionalism and accuracy based on the timestamps, I believe they are in some sort of agreement with all the major news sources therefore allowing them to use their news metadata and sell them. The headlines themselves are generated by authors from those respective news outlets. The summaries however, may have a chance of being generated by AI. Yahoo Finance data are free for anyone and is not accurate in the sense that it does not show both live data and sub-minute ticks. Therefore it leads me to believe that Yahoo Finance also has a deal with major exchanges to display this data for free, to the public. Since it is minute-ticks, this means that the provider, or Yahoo themselves calculated high, low, volume and other fields values to be displayed. 

First the variable of note for yFinance is the ‘Volume’ since we are trying to compare whether news sentiment has an impact on the trade volume of that specific stock.

Example of the data obtained from yFinance `ticker.history(start=start_date, end=end_date)`:

| Date                      |     Open |     High |      Low |    Close |   Volume | Dividends | Stock Splits |
|:--------------------------|---------:|---------:|---------:|---------:|---------:|----------:|-------------:|
| 2026-05-01 00:00:00-04:00 | 278.60329 | 286.95561 | 278.113751 | 279.882141 | 79915400 | 0.0       | 0.0          |

: Sample row returned by yFinance for AAPL {#tbl-yfinance-sample}

This is a sample of what we obtained as a return from yFinance. We specifically will be using the ‘Volume’ column.

Variable name - Volume (integer)
The number of trades conducted on that day is related to that specific ticker.

As for Finnhub, we extracted news data from their api and it returns the following:

```{python}
[
 {
  'category': 'company',
  'datetime': 1622505600,
  'headline': 'Apple announces new iPhone',
  'id': 123456,
  'image': 'https://example.com/image.jpg',
  'related': 'AAPL',
  'source': 'Reuters',
  'summary': 'Apple has announced the release of its new iPhone model, which features a larger display and improved battery life.',
  'url': 'https://www.reuters.com/article/us-apple-iphone-idUSKCN2D90XG'
  },
]
```

This is a sample of what is returned when we called:

```{python}
"https://finnhub.io/api/v1/company-news?symbol={ticker}&from={start_date}&to={end_date}&token={api_key}"
```

We will be using all of these fields for analysis later on, but the main three variables we will be using for our sentiment analysis is the following:'datetime' (unix timestamp - integer) to determine when the article is released. This is crucial because we will eventually be conducting multi-day analysis and we need to separate articles by date. 'headline' (string) and 'summary' (string) are both used to construct 'text' in a later data structure which is used in the sentiment analysis engine. We combine both 'headline' and 'summary' with a ';' as the separator to form 'text'(string). This is because finBERT takes a string of text as input and we want to capture both the summary and the headline as input, without losing any context.

The other variables are used in later supplemental analysis. The main one is 'source' which we will compare which source is the most bullish or bearish and who is reporting on that specific ticker.

Variable name - “datetime” (integer)
The unix timestamp of when the article came out.

Variable name - “headline”(string)
The title of the article.

Variable name - “summary” (string)
The description of the contents of the article.

Variable name - “source” (string)
The name of the news outlet that wrote the article.

Obtaining and combining data from two separate data sources in preparation for analysis is one of the hardest parts of the project. We first obtain the volume data from yFinance using the get_volume_data.

```{python}
def get_volume_data(ticker, start_date, end_date):
    """Fetches the volume of trades conducted on the specified company and date.

    Args:
        ticker (str): Stock ticker symbol
        start_date (str): Start date in the format YYYY-MM-DD
        end_date (str): End date in the format YYYY-MM-DD

    Returns:
        pandas.Series: A series containing the volume of trades for the specified company and date range
    """
    ticker = yf.Ticker(ticker)
    hist = ticker.history(start=start_date, end=end_date)
    return hist["Volume"]
```

This function obtains and returns the trade volume of a given stock for the given time duration. The ticker (example: AAPL) is passed into the yf.Ticker class to obtain the ticker specific information. We then access the history by calling the history() method from the Ticker class, passing in a start and end date to get the volume data from that range.

```{python}
def get_news_company(ticker, start_date, end_date):
    """Fetches news articles from Finnhub for a specific company within a given date range. Returns a list of news articles that has headline, summary, source, and url.

    Args:
        ticker (str): Stock ticker symbol
        start_date (str): Start date in the format YYYY-MM-DD
        end_date (str): End date in the format YYYY-MM-DD

    Returns:
        list: A list of news articles for the specified company and date range in this format:
        [
            {
                'category': 'company',
                'datetime': 1622505600,
                'headline': 'Apple announces new iPhone',
                'id': 123456,
                'image': 'https://example.com/image.jpg',
                'related': 'AAPL',
                'source': 'Reuters',
                'summary': 'Apple has announced the release of its new iPhone model, which features a larger display and improved battery life.',
                'url': 'https://www.reuters.com/article/us-apple-iphone-idUSKCN2D90XG'
            }
        ]
    """
    api_key = os.getenv("FINNHUB_API_KEY")
    url = f"https://finnhub.io/api/v1/company-news?symbol={ticker}&from={start_date}&to={end_date}&token={api_key}"
    r = requests.get(url)

    if r.status_code == 200:
        return r.json()
    else:
        print(f"Error fetching news data: {r.status_code}")
        return None
```

The function above fetches the company headline and summary using the Finnhub API when given a specific ticker symbol, a start and end date. The api requires an api key which is imported with the python_dotenv package. It uses the request function from the requests package to conduct the api request. It also has simple error handling for any unexpected behaviors. The description of the function also provided an example of the return values.

```{python}
def get_news_range(symbol, start_date, end_date):
    """Get the news based on the range of the dates. Increments a day at a time to bypass return limits. Finnhub could only return a limited amount of articles at a time. Therefore we chunked it up to get more dates.

    Args:
        symbol (str): The symbol you are trying to fetch
        start_date (str): Start date in %Y-%m-%d
        end_date (str): End date in %Y-%m-%d

    Returns:
        [dict]: List of all the article metadata
    """
    start = datetime.strptime(start_date, "%Y-%m-%d").date()
    end = datetime.strptime(end_date, "%Y-%m-%d").date()

    all_news = []
    cur = start
    print("Begin fetching news articles:")
    while cur < end:
        day_str = cur.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        chunk = get_news_company(symbol, day_str, day_str) or []
        all_news.extend(chunk)
        cur += timedelta(days=1)
        time.sleep(0.05)
        print("Day:", cur)
       
    return all_news
```

get_news_range extends on the get_news_company function to obtain news articles that are more than a day. This is important because for financial analysis we usually only see the effect over a long period of time, therefore we would require the fetching of a range of dates. Finnhub also has a limit to how many news articles it could send, and for long range dates, it often silently drops those dates.

```{python}
def data_transformation_engine(news_raw, volume):
    """Combines both yFinance volume data and Finnhub article data.
    Finnhub articles use unix time, which needs to be converted to string time.
    Also converts the Series integer data structure in pandas to python primitive int.
    Iterates through both news and volume data inputs and combines them into a single dataset.

    Args:
        news_raw (list(dict)): A giant list of dictionaries containing metadata information for articles release
        volume (pandas.Series): A pandas series of dates and the traded volumes

    Returns:
        _type_:
       
        type dataset:
            key: date strings in the format of "%Y-%m-%d"
            value: Day
           
        type Day:
            'articles': [Article] --> list of articles
            'volume': int
           
        type Article:
            "ticker": str,
            "headline": str,
            "summary": str,
            "text": str,
            "source": str,
            "date": datetime
    """
    dataset = {}
    news = transform_news_data(news_raw)
   
    vol_by_date = {}
    for ts, vol in volume.items():
        date_str = ts.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        vol_by_date[date_str] = int(vol)

    for article in news:
        date = article['date']
        if date not in vol_by_date:
            continue
        if date not in dataset:
            dataset[date] = {
                'articles': [article],
                'volume': vol_by_date[date],
            }
        else:
            dataset[date]['articles'].append(article)
   
    # print("Verification:")
    # for d in dataset:
    #     print(d, len(dataset[d]['articles']), dataset[d]['volume'])
       
    return dataset
```

The snippet above is code from the multiday_financial_news_sentiment_pipeline where the extraction code is obtained and combined with the data transformation engine. The transform_news_data returns the cleaned, and processed article information containing the ‘text’ field which is the combination of ‘headline’ and ‘summary’ fields.

```{txt}
vol_by_date = {}
for ts, vol in volume.items():
    date_str = ts.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
    vol_by_date[date_str] = int(vol)
```

This snippet changes the string time to the proper format so it could be the same as the volume dates. 

```{txt}
for article in news:
    date = article['date']
    if date not in vol_by_date:
        continue
    if date not in dataset:
        dataset[date] = {
            'articles': [article],
            'volume': vol_by_date[date],
        }
    else:
        dataset[date]['articles'].append(article)
```

Next, we generate the “day” data structure by constructing a dictionary that has two fields:

```{txt}
dataset[date] = {
    'articles': [article],
    'volume': vol_by_date[date],
}
```

This is the basic structure of a “day”. It contains a list of all the articles, and has the volume for the day. The ‘date’ serves as the primary key that connects the two datasets following the format "%Y-%m-%d".

Data_transformation_engine function takes in inputs from both get_news_company and get_volume_data formats and then merges them into a single data structure. The data is finally returned as a big structure like the following:

```
type dataset:
    key: date strings in the format of "%Y-%m-%d"
    value: Day
    
type Day:
    'articles': [Article] --> list of articles
    'volume': int
    
type Article:
    "ticker": str,
    "headline": str,
    "summary": str,
    "text": str,
    "source": str,
    "date": datetime
```
The main results of the merging of the code returns a list of “days”. This is so that the analysis could iterate over this later for visualization and ease of calculation. Visualizations use daily data as the bars in the bar chart, so having each of them in lists assists in decreasing the complexity later down the line.

# Transformation Processes

```{python}
def transform_news_data(news_data):

   transformed = []

   for article in news_data:

       headline = article.get("headline", "")
       summary = article.get("summary", "")

       # combine text
       text = f"{headline}. {summary}"

       # cleaning
       text = text.replace("\xa0", " ")
       text = text.replace("&#39;", "'")
       text = " ".join(text.split())
       text = text.encode("utf-8", "ignore").decode("utf-8")

       # convert timestamp
       date = datetime.fromtimestamp(
           article["datetime"]
       ).strftime("%Y-%m-%d")

       transformed.append({
           "ticker": article.get("related"),
           "headline": headline,
           "summary": summary,
           "text": text,
           "source": article.get("source"),
           "date": date
       })

   return transformed

def get_news_range(symbol, start_date, end_date):
   """Get the news based on the range of the dates. Increments a day at a time to bypass return limits. Finnhub could only return a limited amount of articles at a time. Therefore we chunked it up to get more dates.

   Args:
       symbol (str): The symbol you are trying to fetch
       start_date (str): Start date in %Y-%m-%d
       end_date (str): End date in %Y-%m-%d

   Returns:
       [dict]: List of all the article metadata
   """
   start = datetime.strptime(start_date, "%Y-%m-%d").date()
   end = datetime.strptime(end_date, "%Y-%m-%d").date()

   all_news = []
   cur = start
   print("Begin fetching news articles:")
   while cur < end:
       day_str = cur.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
       chunk = get_news_company(symbol, day_str, day_str) or []
       all_news.extend(chunk)
       cur += timedelta(days=1)
       time.sleep(0.05)
       print("Day:", cur)
      
   return all_news

def data_transformation_engine(news_raw, volume):
   """Combines both yFinance volume data and Finnhub article data.
   Finnhub articles uses unix time, which needs to be converted to string time.
   Also converts the Series integer data structure in pandas to python primitive int.
   Iterates through both news and volume data inputs and combines them into a single dataset.

   Args:
       news_raw (list(dict)): A giant list of dictionaries containing metadata information for articles release
       volume (pandas.Series): A pandas series of dates and the traded volumes

   Returns:
       _type_:
      
       type dataset:
           key: date strings in the format of "%Y-%m-%d"
           value: Day
          
       type Day:
           'articles': [Article] --> list of articles
           'volume': int
          
       type Article:
           "ticker": str,
           "headline": str,
           "summary": str,
           "text": str,
           "source": str,
           "date": datetime
   """
   dataset = {}
   news = transform_news_data(news_raw)
  
   vol_by_date = {}
   for ts, vol in volume.items():
       date_str = ts.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
       vol_by_date[date_str] = int(vol)

   for article in news:
       date = article['date']
       if date not in vol_by_date:
           continue
       if date not in dataset:
           dataset[date] = {
               'articles': [article],
               'volume': vol_by_date[date],
           }
       else:
           dataset[date]['articles'].append(article)
  
   # print("Verification:")
   # for d in dataset:
   #     print(d, len(dataset[d]['articles']), dataset[d]['volume'])
      
   return dataset
      

def sentimental_analysis_engine(sen_engine, dataset):

   for day in dataset:
       txt_buffer = [txt['text'] for txt in dataset[day]['articles']]
       dataset[day]['sentiment_results'] = sen_engine(txt_buffer)
      

def multiday_financial_news_sentiment_pipeline(symbol, start_date, end_date, sen_engine):
   """Multiday news sentiment engine. Uses FinBERT as the sentimental analysis. Obtains daily volume data from yfinance and processed news data from Finnhub.

   Args:
       symbol (str): The symbol you are trying to fetch
       start_date (str): Start date in %Y-%m-%d
       end_date (str): End date in %Y-%m-%d
      
   Returns:
       variable: transformed_dataset
  
       type dict Transformed_Dataset:
           key: date strings in the format of "%Y-%m-%d"
           value: type Day
          
       variable: final_dataset:
      
       type list Final_Dataset:
           items: type Article
          
       type dict Day:
           'articles': [Article] --> list of articles
           'volume': int
          
       type dict Article:
           "ticker": str,
           "headline": str,
           "summary": str,
           "text": str,
           "source": str,
           "date": datetime,
           "sentiment": int,
           "sen_confidence": int
          
       variable:
          
   """
   # Extraction
   news_raw = get_news_range(symbol, start_date, end_date)
   vol = get_volume_data(symbol, start_date, end_date)
  
   # Transformation
   transformed_dataset = data_transformation_engine(news_raw, vol)
  
   # Load
   sentimental_analysis_engine(sen_engine, transformed_dataset)
  
   # Build final dataset
   # print(clean_data.keys()) --> Days
   # print(clean_data['2026-05-01'].keys()) --> {'articles', 'volume', 'sentiment_results'}

   final_dataset = []
  
   for day in transformed_dataset:
       for article, sentiment in zip(transformed_dataset[day]['articles'], transformed_dataset[day]['sentiment_results']):

           article["sentiment"] = sentiment["label"]
           article["sen_confidence"] = sentiment["score"]

           final_dataset.append(article)
           # print(article.keys()) --> {'ticker', 'headline', 'summary', 'text', 'source', 'date', 'sentiment', 'sen_confidence'}
          
  
  
   return [final_dataset, transformed_dataset]


if __name__ == "__main__":

    # Load finbert model
    finbert = pipeline(
        "text-classification",
        model="ProsusAI/finbert",
        tokenizer="ProsusAI/finbert"
    )

    # Set start and end dates, in our case we will use May 01 - May 02
    start_date = "2026-05-01"
    end_date = "2026-05-08"
    symbol = "AAPL"

    # Obtain news regarding a specific symbol (in our case it will be Apple)
    [final_dataset, results] = multiday_financial_news_sentiment_pipeline(symbol, start_date, end_date, finbert)

    # OUTPUT
    df = pd.DataFrame(final_dataset)
    df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])

    for row in final_dataset:
        print("\n-------------------")
        print(f"Headline: {row['headline']}")
        print(f"Sentiment: {row['sentiment']}")
        print(f"Confidence: {row['sen_confidence']:.2f}")
        print(f"Date: {row['date']}")
```

The transformation stage focused on converting raw financial news data into a clean and structured dataset suitable for sentiment analysis and exploratory data analysis (EDA). The raw data was extracted from the Finnhub API and initially contained nested JSON objects with unnecessary metadata, inconsistent formatting, and timestamp values that were not human-readable. Several transformation steps were performed to improve data quality, consistency, and usability.
First, relevant attributes were selected from the raw API response, including the stock ticker symbol, article headline, summary, source, and publication date. This reduced unnecessary information and ensured the final dataset only contained variables relevant to the project objectives.

Next, the headline and summary fields were combined into a single text field. This was done because sentiment analysis models generally perform better with more contextual information rather than short headlines alone. Combining both fields allowed the sentiment model to capture additional financial context from the article summaries.

Text-cleaning transformations were then applied to standardize the article content. Special characters such as non-breaking spaces and HTML escape characters were removed or replaced to improve readability and prevent encoding issues during analysis. Extra whitespace was also removed using string normalization techniques. Additionally, UTF-8 encoding and decoding were applied to handle malformed characters and ensure consistent text formatting across all articles.

The Unix timestamp values provided by Finnhub were transformed into human-readable dates using Python’s datetime module. This made the dataset easier to interpret and enabled later temporal analysis of sentiment trends over time.

After cleaning and restructuring the data, the transformed text was processed using the FinBERT sentiment analysis model from Hugging Face Transformers. FinBERT is specifically trained on financial text, making it more appropriate for financial news sentiment classification than general-purpose sentiment models. Each article received a sentiment label (positive, negative, or neutral) along with a confidence score representing the model’s certainty.

Finally, all transformed and enriched records were stored in a list and converted into a Pandas DataFrame. This final structured dataset served as the project’s final data product and was later used for descriptive and bias-related exploratory data analysis.

The transformation decisions were guided by the project goal of analyzing relationships between financial news sentiment and stock-related discussion trends. Ensuring clean, standardized, and context-rich text data was important for improving the reliability of the sentiment analysis results and supporting accurate downstream analysis.


# Analysis & Evaluation
The text in this section includes:
- descriptions of all variables
- noteworthy patterns,
- including discussion of potential sources of bias or unbalanced
patterns
You are encouraged to structure this major section into subsections as you
wish, and to break up and interweave code chunks, to help with
organization and readability.
```{python}
# The code chunk(s) in this section should include:
# - descriptive stats for all relevant variables
# - descriptive stat(s) for any relevant relationships
# - visualizations for all relevant variables
# - visualizations for any relevant relationships
# - code for exploring potential sources of bias, including missing data,
```

# Data Product & Guide
The text in this section includes:
- description and "user's guide" for the final data product
- general info and description on each variable/column in the data
product
- note: the descriptive stats, etc. should already be in the Analysis
section, but if there are any new/transformed variables that were not
in that section, those should have descriptive stats and plots here
```{python}
# The code chunk(s) in this section should include:
# - The code needed to "finalize" or "export" the final data product.
```
